speaker: interviewer
text: --Ladislav Jung. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Tape number three. And you were telling us about your arrival to Buchenwald. And this is at what time of year? What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was winter. That was 1945. And there was already a nightmare where we were handled by SS. And when, like I said, when we arrived we were not sure is a destruction camp or there is gas or whatever. So when I arrived and-- and I try to communicate.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they-- they ask, who can cut hair. Who is a barber? [LAUGHS] So I raised my hand. So I thought I will save my skin. And I-- I ask, is there any destruction, will-- they will gas us or not. And in between I had to shear the hair off. And the fellow [LAUGHS] under me, who I was shaving his head off was screaming his head off. I didn't have any experience till I find out this everything is OK. I jump-- jumped down and I mix with the other crowd and asking where is this barber. Where is this barber?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I just went into the shower. And-- and we got showered. And our clothes, and everything, what we had, was gone. And we got their ready outfit, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] bands with stripes and wooden shoes and a shirt what was with number. And we-- my number was 115 [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 834. And there was-- we had to sew it on or pin it down right here. And after all this, we were going to barracks. For a few days, we were in upper camp, what was a very nice camp in hell, how they say. Only later on, they put us in a camp in lower camp, where there were-- Upper camp was political prisoners and-- and people who were still in good condition healthwise and so forth. And when there came orders and put us in the lower camp, there was terrible-- there were people sick with typhoid, with dysentery, with diarrhea, with-- people couldn't control and just bleed to death through their through their stomach and-- so that was a terrible nightmare.

speaker: interviewer
text: So for a while, I-- I met some Russian prisoners only they were from, like, Uzbekistan and-- and so forth. And somehow I could organize and get a little bit extra soup, what was warm water, what-- with nothing in it. And-- How did you manage to get the extra?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russian prisoners, I met them. I befriended some of them. And and that was terrible, while we didn't get any bread and any food. That was end of the-- end of-- ending of the war. And I later on was transferred to a barracks where there were kids, kids, young people from we say, from 10 or nine till about 16. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there 15. No. I was more than. One second-- '45 is-- yeah, about. And over there we got a little bit better treatment. We didn't have to stay all night in frost outside and counting and-- for punishment people were flying-- falling like flies. Every morning you got up. If you could sleep a little bit, you got up and you saw outside big [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] piles of dead people, sometime 50, sometime 100, sometime even more people. What they throw there on the pile, they were just skin and bone. And they were taken on wagon drawn by the prisoners to crematorium and burned. Or some of them just didn't have time to burn. They just were in ditches there. So that was a very, very bad and depressing time over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever singled out for punishment or beating?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I-- I was not singled out for punishment. There were lots of people they just didn't like their look. So whatever. They just killed them or beat them to death or--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Yes. Many times. Many times.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe an incident?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, mainly, I go back to-- to Deblin. For instance, my friend run away with his friend and he was caught in Poland on the railroad. They shot him on the spot. And then they came to our camp. And we had to stay in the lines in, like, appell. And-- and the-- the Gendarmes, they were coming up and looking on the people and say, you step out. You come step out. And he was there with a vicious German shepherd. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and they took few people to dig ditches. So we know what is waiting for them. And they just put them up and they shoot there for each prisoner. For each one he run away. There were two. So they took 20 people, 10 for each. And they just put him there and shoot him. And the dogs were biting him. And-- and he was-- he had a whip, like a bullwhip with the lead on the end. And-- and just whip him till the blood was coming through from everywhere. And what he didn't whip, the dogs tear him-- tear him apart. And they killed them. And then the-- the Gendarme, they said, well, the grave is not full yet. So they took-- he said, we can do it quicker. So they took few more people and just cold blooded, shoot them. And there were other incidents where they found money some-- on the one of the fellows. And-- and for punishment, they said, all. They check all our belongings in the place where we used to, [INAUDIBLE] in barracks. And then they hang few people, just they were hanging there. And also, they let them hang there for-- for like day or two. And when we came from work, we marched by, we see them hanging. There was some other incident where they didn't like the man. They just whip him to death. So life-- life was worthless in those--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in--

speaker: interviewee
text: --camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: --all in Deblin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Deblin. And then in Buchenwald of course. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were thousands of people who fall like flies.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Buchenwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I-- when we already heard bombing in Buchenwald, when they were bombing Leipzig, and so forth, we knew where-- over there, that we were pretty cut off of the outside life. Only we know that the Americans are-- are coming closer.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then, I-- I was transferred to the kids' barracks and life was better. But you didn't have any punishment. Or you didn't have stay so long in appell outside in freezing temperatures. So this way was easier. Only we-- the last days we didn't get food at all nor even the warm water, dirt-- dirtied warm water with-- with rotten peels of potatoes or something like that. Did you know where your father was at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this point, when the-- when the front was coming closer, they were start to taking transports out of-- of Buchenwald and chasing by foot. And there was time when my-- I told my father. He was chosen next day to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I told my father, why we don't switch one number from the dead people and-- and put it on or stick it on. And-- and this way, you eliminate go on transport. Then, somehow, you can still eliminate to-- to part.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father, he said, no. I am afraid that may be we can go together. And I says, look here. I don't know what would be the end. Only I don't want to go on transport while I heard they just went out of the camp and they were killed. So I says, I don't want commit suicide. And we will part if you afraid to do that. And there was one of the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] hard-- hardest [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] decisions [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] of my life. And he was eliminated on the march by the Czech borders in [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And we had hard time where we didn't get any food. And there were one time I got hold of rotten potato peels. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And-- and this I ate. And I-- then they were chasing people out without any discrimination from the barracks, who they could caught, and take him and try to shoot him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one day when they came, I ripped off a plank from the barracks. And I tried to hide on the roof. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and some of the boys saw me crawling up. So everybody start to crawl up till the ceiling came down.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I tried to look for other provisions. Once I open a-- a-- from a sewer line, there was on this side outside in the field like cover. And I thought maybe I will, in emergency, climb down and try to save my life. Then, I also this time, befriended a friend who were transported from Terezin to-- to Buchenwald. And we become befriended. And there were last days of the-- before our liberation. His name was Pavel Koen He was a-- he was, after our liberation for-- till now, he was a reporter for the Voice of Free Europe all those years. And I met him after 50 years. And we were counting. We were sitting on the piece of land there overlooking the barracks and the electrical wires at the towers--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the days leading up to liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: The days before liberation. And we were eating the roots of grass. And there was nothing to eat. And we say, well, how many hours still we can survive without-- without anything to eat? And we saw some rhubarb. There was a field down by the wires. And we saw some people running down and eating raw rhubarb. Well, you eat anything what was-- could be eatable. Matter-- matter of fact, there were also stories and-- there was even cannibalism between the-- between the prisoners. And when we saw the people going down by the towers, they were just-- they shoot on them. So we didn't choose to go there. And then, one day we wake up. And-- and Americans came in and liberated. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So there was happy occasion to see the American flag. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And then we were liberated, we stayed for a few days. I don't remember how long. And we were transported back to Czechoslovakia. I never went from beginning to the town where I was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back a moment here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: At liberation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you just describe--

speaker: interviewee
text: The liberation?

speaker: interviewer
text: --a little more how you felt?

speaker: interviewee
text: That felt very good. The-- the life was fantastic. There was lots of joy. And-- and the American soldiers marched in. They didn't believe of the destruction of people-- how one human being can do something to the other. And then they brought also some Germans, people from nearby, from-- from Leipzig and Buchenwald. They brought some mayors and showed them. Oh, we didn't know what's going on. Of course, some of them don't. And lots of them did. While probably the SS brag about it and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: So lots of people knew about it. And they were, some of them, they thought, OK. And some also-- some SS people, they told them to-- to help to keep the corpses and clean them up. And-- and-- and was forced to do that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And some Germans, when we came in town, they were friendly. Some of them were still very unfriendly too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back to-- to when you had to say goodbye to your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- do you remember the last things that you said between your father and yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told him this. Look here. We had two times opportunity to run away, and you were afraid. And this time, I don't want to risk it, while I know there'd be-- there'd be a certain death. So I said to him, please, try to do-- take the effort to do it. And he was just afraid to do it. And well, of course, he was maybe much wiser than me. So from different standpoint, I was still young and a kid. And so there was two different point your views. And-- so that was very hard. It was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you returned to your barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: And you returned to your barrack, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I returned to my barrack, back. That was terrible. Only then, we were very hardened people. We, we the emotions, were killed in us almost. So it was, still, that was very hard. Where you-- you dealed with death every day, in and out. And you-- you were, actually, after so many years, you-- you were completely different person without emotions and without any feelings. So it was very hard. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Only [? by-- ?] I came back to Czechoslovakia. We were transported in-- in buses. And before I go back, some people were so dehydrated. And their stomach, everything, was shrunk. This when they came, and they tried to feed the people when they didn't have the experience with food. They give them the food. And people just couldn't take it. And they just died.

speaker: interviewer
text: So only that was a fantastic experience. When you were hungry for-- for months, and some of them for years, you dreamed of-- to have loaf of bread. And the [INAUDIBLE] on top of it. Only if you would do that, probably you wouldn't survive on your stomach and you couldn't take it. So-- Do you remember the first food you were given then, after liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got a C ration from American soldier. I got a-- those cakes. They were in a green round [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] container. That was like the-- oh, how you call it, the cake, that make it on Christmas-- Fruitcake.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fruitcake.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and then-- then we got-- first we got C rations. And while there was no possibilities. And-- and bread and all kinds of goodies. So we slowly, we, between the prisoners, we told each other, hey, start it slowly and slowly till-- till you get used to normal food.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: We go-- we were bussed to-- to Czechoslovakia. And they asked-- they asked [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where we-- from Buchenwald they asked us where we would like to go. Some people choose go to their home-- homeland. And of course I'm from Czechoslovakia. And I know I had the uncle and-- in Pilsen. And also a village near Pilsen. So-- and before-- before World War II, he was an officer in Czechoslovakian army. So I went to look for him. While I know he's-- he was not Jewish, only he had to hide too while his wife was Jewish. So he went to hiding. And-- and he was in Pilsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I met him there. And he was already officer in the army. And so he took me under his wing. And we waited for news who-- who came back. So he-- his wife was last year-- While she was protected through him, only then the last year she was in prison. And she was in-- in Terezin. And we didn't have any news then through the Red Cross. We know she's alive only she's sick. She had typhoid. So then-- then he give me some roof over the head. I arrived with German army boots. One [LAUGHS] one was two sizes bigger than the other. And I had still the stripe clothing on. Well, I didn't have nothing else to wear. So when I arrived, and he brought me to a family, to his friends, and they tried to burn the clothing while they-- they were afraid the clothing may be infested with [LAUGHS] who know what.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember there was a nice apartment. And they give me a good bath, good scrub down. And they've give me some normal civilian clothing. So then I slept in a bed with sheets and white sheets. And think I told I've been in-- in heaven. And I got some soup, warm soup, and some normal dinner. So that was a real good treat. And then we went to look for the aunt. And he-- he had opportunity while he had transportation. He was a high officer in the Czechoslovakian army. So he had different people working under him and so forth and had a car to his disposition.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then he got the news this aunt is in somewhere in a hospital. And we picked up the aunt. And we got reunited. So this-- this is the only aunt who-- who returned from concentration camp. And-- Can you tell us their names, your aunt and uncle?

speaker: interviewee
text: The uncle is Vlaclav Slama and the-- the aunt is Alizabeta Slama, born Klein. And after war, I was a war orphan. And she took me under her wing. And she took care of me, how long I needed to, till I went into Israel from Czechoslovakia. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you live with the aunt and uncle?

speaker: interviewee
text: I live-- lived there about a year and half to two years with them. And I went-- they give me the opportunity and the roof over the head and food. So it was not easy, of course. Only I could go back to school and also to learn mine-- my trade.
